design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate scrolling vertical battlefield, avoid enemy fire patterns, destroy waves of enemies and boss formations
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Bullet hell avoidance combined with offensive resource management (power-up collection)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Pilot advanced fighter through overwhelming enemy forces with escalating mechanical complexity
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Arcade-rooted shmup that prioritizes pattern recognition and reflexive dodging over narrative; demands mastery through repetition and memorization of enemy wave formations

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives with continue system typical of arcade ports
difficulty.continues_system: Continue mechanic present, standard for Genesis arcade conversions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal; difficulty is fixed by design. Player agency expressed through execution precision and pattern anticipation rather than adaptive mechanics

progression.upgrade_system: Power-up collection during levels (weapons, shields, temporary invulnerability implied by arcade shmup conventions)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential stage progression through completion; no branching paths
replayability_hooks: Mastery of enemy patterns enables higher difficulty engagement
Different power-up routes create micro-variations in playstyle

world_building_techniques: Enemy design implies sci-fi conflict setting (fighter aircraft vs alien/mechanical forces)
Visual progression from earthbound to space environments (typical arcade convention)

boss_design.philosophy: Arcade-traditional design: large mechanical/alien constructs with distinct attack patterns, screen-dominating presence, phase-based HP depletion

danger_signals: Escalating enemy density and projectile patterns indicate approaching boss encounters
Visual color/design shift (mechanical vs organic enemies) signals thematic progression
environmental_storytelling: Terrain transitions (ground to sky to space) imply military/sci-fi conflict escalation
Enemy formation coherence suggests organized opposition rather than random chaos
